terms.definition: How big a vibration is. A bigger amplitude makes a louder sound.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: Hit a drum harder and the skin moves further up and down — a bigger amplitude means a louder sound.
terms.common_errors: Mixing up amplitude with frequency. Amplitude decides loudness; frequency decides pitch.

terms.definition: The unit used to measure how loud a sound is. A whisper is about 30 decibels; a shouting crowd is about 100.
terms.subject: Science
terms.etymology: Named after Alexander Graham Bell; 'deci-' means one tenth.
terms.example_usage: A sound-level app on a tablet shows the decibels in the classroom.
terms.common_errors: Thinking a 60-decibel sound is twice as loud as a 30-decibel sound. The decibel scale is not linear — each extra 10 dB is roughly twice as loud.

terms.definition: A musical instrument made of a stretched skin. Hitting the skin makes it vibrate and produces a sound.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: Placing rice on a drum skin and tapping the drum shows the vibration — the rice jumps up and down.
terms.common_errors: Thinking the drum body makes the sound. It is the stretched skin that vibrates, not the wood or metal body.

terms.definition: Very quiet and hard to hear. Sounds become fainter as you move further from the source.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: A ticking clock is clear in the same room but faint from the hall.
terms.common_errors: Thinking faint sounds are disappearing. They are still there — the sound energy is just spreading out over more space.

terms.definition: How many times something vibrates each second. The higher the frequency, the higher the pitch of the sound.
terms.subject: Science
terms.etymology: Latin *frequentia*, meaning crowded or happening often.
terms.example_usage: A tuning fork marked '440 Hz' vibrates 440 times every second.
terms.common_errors: Confusing frequency with loudness. A loud sound can have a low frequency and a quiet sound can have a high frequency.

terms.definition: To sense a sound with your ear. Hearing happens when sound waves make your eardrum vibrate.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: I can hear the tuning fork even though the vibration is too fast to see clearly.
terms.common_errors: Thinking hearing works because something reaches the ear. It is really the vibration of the eardrum that creates hearing.

terms.definition: A high sound has fast vibrations and a high pitch. See *pitch*.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: A bird's whistle is a high sound; a lion's roar is a low sound.
terms.common_errors: Confusing high (pitch) with high up (position). Sound is about the speed of vibration, not where the source is.

terms.definition: An object designed to make musical sounds when you play it.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: A guitar, a drum and a recorder are all instruments that work by making something vibrate.
terms.common_errors: Thinking only musical instruments vibrate. Every sound source vibrates — voices, traffic and waves on a beach.

terms.definition: How long something is. A longer vibrating object produces a lower-pitched sound.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: A long ruler hanging over the desk edge vibrates slowly and makes a low sound; a short overhang makes a high sound.
terms.common_errors: Thinking length only affects volume. In sound, length mainly affects pitch.

terms.definition: A long vibrating object has more material to move, vibrates slowly, and makes a lower pitch.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: A long ruler over the desk edge makes a lower sound than a short overhang.
terms.common_errors: Thinking 'long' only describes the sound itself. In sound lessons, long usually describes the vibrating object.

terms.definition: A loose string has little tension and vibrates slowly, making a lower pitch.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: If a guitar string is too loose, it makes a dull, low-pitched sound.
terms.common_errors: Thinking loose sounds quieter. Looseness affects pitch, not volume.

terms.definition: A loud sound has a big vibration (large amplitude). See *volume*.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: A loud drumbeat makes the drum skin move a long way up and down.
terms.common_errors: Thinking loud means high-pitched. Loud and high are different: loud is about size of vibration, high is about speed.

terms.definition: A low sound has slow vibrations and a low pitch. See *pitch*.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: A bass drum makes a low sound; a triangle makes a high sound.
terms.common_errors: Confusing low (pitch) with quiet. A low sound can be very loud — a bass drum is both.

terms.definition: The material a sound travels through — solid, liquid or gas. Sound cannot travel through a vacuum because there is no medium.
terms.subject: Science
terms.etymology: Latin *medium*, meaning middle or in between.
terms.example_usage: Sound travels faster through a solid medium like wood than through a gas like air.
terms.common_errors: Thinking 'medium' means middle-sized, like a medium T-shirt. In science it means the material a wave passes through.

terms.definition: To make a sound quieter by absorbing some of its vibrations. Thick materials muffle sound.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: Wrapping a ringing alarm clock in a blanket muffles the sound so you hardly hear it.
terms.common_errors: Thinking muffling blocks sound completely. It absorbs some of the sound energy — the sound is still there, just quieter.

terms.definition: A single musical sound with one definite pitch.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: Each bar of a glockenspiel plays a different note when you strike it.
terms.common_errors: Thinking 'note' is about loudness. A note is defined by its pitch — you can play the same note loudly or softly.

terms.definition: How high or low a sound is. Fast vibrations make a high pitch; slow vibrations make a low pitch.
terms.subject: Science
terms.etymology: The musical sense of *pitch* dates from the 16th century.
terms.example_usage: A short ruler vibrates faster than a long one, so it makes a higher pitch.
terms.common_errors: Confusing pitch with volume. Pitch is high or low; volume is loud or quiet. They are independent properties of sound.

terms.definition: A quiet sound has a small vibration (small amplitude). See *volume*.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: A whisper is a quiet sound because the vocal cords vibrate only a little.
terms.common_errors: Thinking quiet means low-pitched. A quiet whistle is still high-pitched.

terms.definition: A short vibrating object has less material to move, vibrates quickly, and makes a higher pitch.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: Shortening the ruler overhang raises the pitch of the sound it makes when twanged.
terms.common_errors: Thinking short sounds are always quiet. Short refers to the vibrating object, not the loudness of the sound.

terms.definition: Something you hear. A sound is made when an object vibrates, and the vibrations travel through a material to your ears.
terms.subject: Science
terms.etymology: Old French *son*, from Latin *sonus*, meaning noise.
terms.example_usage: The drum makes a loud sound when you hit the skin and it vibrates.
terms.common_errors: Thinking a sound is a thing the vibrating object sends out. The sound is the vibration itself being passed from one particle to the next.

terms.definition: How loud a sound is, usually measured in decibels.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: We measured the sound level in the lunch hall using a sound-level app.
terms.common_errors: Using 'sound level' to mean pitch. Sound level is about loudness, not how high or low a sound is.

terms.definition: How fast something moves. Sound travels at about 340 metres per second in air, but faster through solids.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: The speed of sound in air is about 340 m/s; in water it is about 1500 m/s.
terms.common_errors: Thinking louder sounds travel faster than quiet ones. Volume does not change the speed of sound in the same material.

terms.definition: A tight line of wire, gut or nylon on a musical instrument. Plucking it makes it vibrate and produce a sound.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: A guitar string makes a different pitch depending on its length and how tight it is.
terms.common_errors: Thinking strings only make sound when plucked. They also vibrate when bowed (violin) or struck (piano).

terms.definition: Two cups joined by a tight string. Sound vibrations travel along the string from one cup to the other.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: Two pupils with a string telephone can hear each other across a noisy room, if the string stays tight.
terms.common_errors: Thinking the sound travels through the air inside the string. It travels through the string itself, which is why the string must be tight.

terms.definition: How tight something is pulled. A string with more tension vibrates faster and produces a higher-pitched sound.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: Tightening a guitar string increases its tension and raises the pitch.
terms.common_errors: Confusing tension with thickness. Both affect pitch but in different ways: more tension gives a higher pitch; more thickness gives a lower pitch.

terms.definition: A thick string or skin vibrates slowly and makes a lower pitch.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: The thick bass string on a guitar makes the lowest note.
terms.common_errors: Thinking thick sounds louder. Thickness changes pitch, not volume.

terms.definition: How thick something is. A thicker string or skin usually vibrates more slowly and makes a lower pitch.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: The thickest strings on a guitar play the lowest notes.
terms.common_errors: Thinking thickness affects how loud the sound is. It mainly changes the pitch, not the volume.

terms.definition: A thin string or skin vibrates quickly and makes a higher pitch.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: The thinnest string on a guitar makes the highest note.
terms.common_errors: Thinking thin sounds are always quiet. Thinness changes pitch, not volume.

terms.definition: A tight string has a lot of tension and vibrates quickly, making a higher pitch.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: Tighten the guitar string and the note gets higher; loosen it and the note gets lower.
terms.common_errors: Thinking tight sounds are always loud. Tightness affects pitch, not volume.

terms.definition: To pass something on from one place to another. Particles transmit the vibration of a sound from one to the next.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: A string telephone transmits your voice along the string to the other cup.
terms.common_errors: Thinking transmit means send — like posting a letter. In sound, each particle only pushes its neighbour; the particles themselves do not travel.

terms.definition: To move from one place to another. Sound travels as a wave from the vibrating object to your ear.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: The sound of the bell travels through the air and reaches the other side of the playground.
terms.common_errors: Thinking sound travels fastest through air. Sound actually travels fastest through solids, slower through liquids, and slowest through gases.

terms.definition: A metal instrument with two prongs that vibrates at one exact pitch when tapped.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: Strike the tuning fork and dip it in water — you'll see the prongs vibrating by the splashes they make.
terms.common_errors: Tapping a tuning fork on a hard surface, which can chip it. Always tap it on the heel of your hand or a rubber block.

terms.definition: A completely empty space with no air or any other material. Sound cannot travel through a vacuum.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: Space is almost a vacuum, which is why astronauts cannot hear each other shouting without radios.
terms.common_errors: Thinking of a vacuum as the cleaning appliance. In science a vacuum is any space with no particles in it.

terms.definition: To move back and forth very quickly. When an object vibrates it produces a sound.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: Twang a ruler hanging over the edge of a desk and watch it vibrate.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting that everyday sounds (speech, traffic) come from things vibrating even when you can't see the vibration.

terms.definition: A fast back-and-forth movement. All sounds are caused by something vibrating.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: You can feel the vibration of your throat by putting a hand on your neck while humming.
terms.common_errors: Thinking vibration only happens in guitar strings or drums. Every sound — voices, traffic, wind — comes from something vibrating.

terms.definition: Two small folds in your throat that vibrate to make the sounds of your voice.
terms.subject: Science
terms.example_usage: Put your hand on your throat and hum — you can feel your vocal cords vibrating.
terms.common_errors: Thinking only musical instruments make sound through vibration. Your voice works the same way — by vibrating vocal cords.

terms.definition: How loud a sound is. A bigger vibration (larger amplitude) makes a bigger volume.
terms.subject: Science
terms.etymology: Latin *volumen*, meaning a roll or a thing rolled up.
terms.example_usage: Turn up the volume on a speaker and the cone vibrates further in and out.
terms.common_errors: Mixing up 'volume' in sound (loudness) with 'volume' in maths (the 3D space something fills). Same word, two completely different meanings.

terms.definition: A pattern of vibration that travels from one place to another. A sound wave carries the vibration from the source to your ear.
terms.subject: Science
terms.etymology: Old English *wafian*, to move to and fro.
terms.example_usage: When you hit a drum, a sound wave spreads outwards through the air in all directions.
terms.common_errors: Picturing a sound wave as something physical moving across the room. The air particles themselves do not travel — they only pass the vibration along.
